fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Rijke defines an identity system on an element a:A to consist of a family of types \mathcal {I} over A equipped with the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each family P over \mathcal {I}, a dependent function J_{\mathcal {I}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}P{\mathopen {}\left (x,q\right )\mathclose {}} and a dependent function \beta _{\mathcal {I}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{J_{\mathcal {I}}P\,p\,a\,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}} =_{P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}} {p}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Introduction to homotopy type theory
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{rijke-2022, title={Introduction to Homotopy Type Theory}, author={Rijke, Egbert}, year={2022}, eprint={2212.11082}, archivePrefix={arXiv}, primaryClass={math.LO}, }

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: This is an introductory textbook to univalent mathematics and homotopy type theory, a mathematical foundation that takes advantage of the structural nature of mathematical definitions and constructions. It is common in mathematical practice to consider equivalent objects to be the same, for example, to identify isomorphic groups. In set theory it is not possible to make this common practice formal. For example, there are as many distinct trivial groups in set theory as there are distinct singleton sets. Type theory, on the other hand, takes a more structural approach to the foundations of mathematics that accommodates the univalence axiom. This, however, requires us to rethink what it means for two objects to be equal. This textbook introduces the reader to Martin-Löf’s dependent type theory, to the central concepts of univalent mathematics, and shows the reader how to do mathematics from a univalent point of view. Over 200 exercises are included to train the reader in type theoretic reasoning. The book is entirely self-contained, and in particular no prior familiarity with type theory or homotopy theory is assumed.
Rijke defines an identity system on an element a:A to consist of a family of types \mathcal {I} over A equipped with the following data:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each family P over \mathcal {I}, a dependent function J_{\mathcal {I}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (x:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {I}x\right )\mathclose {}}}}P{\mathopen {}\left (x,q\right )\mathclose {}} and a dependent function \beta _{\mathcal {I}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{J_{\mathcal {I}}P\,p\,a\,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}} =_{P{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}} {p}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {I} be an identity system on an element a:A and let B be a family of types over A. A dependent identity system over \mathcal {I} on an element b:Ba in the family B consists of a family of types \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (x,q\right )\mathclose {}} over Bx for each x:A,q:\mathcal {I}x such that \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{I}\right )\mathclose {}} is equipped with the structure of an identity system on b : Ba. In particular, we have:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each family P over \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{I}\right )\mathclose {}}, a dependent function J_{\mathcal {J}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{P{\mathopen {}\left (y,q\right )\mathclose {}}} and a dependent function \beta _{\mathcal {J}}P:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}} {J_{\mathcal {J}}p\,b\,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}} =_{P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}} {p}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: One of the most common tasks when working in univalent foundations is to characterize the path space of a given type, i.e. establish a structure identity principle. Formalized SIP proofs often follow a baroque style involving identity systems and the even more complex displayed identity systems, etc. that sometimes obscure the very simple informal reasoning behind a given structure identity principle; the proposal is to develop a formal proof language to better reflect this informal reasoning, backed by a suite of lemmas and tactics.
To understand what I mean, let us consider how we might characterize the path spaces of morphisms in a co-slice A\downarrow \mathcal {U}.

Let A be a type; an A-algebra X is defined to be a type {\mathopen {}\left \lvert X\right \rvert \mathclose {}} equipped with a function {A}\xrightarrow {{\alpha _A}}{{\mathopen {}\left \lvert X\right \rvert \mathclose {}}}. A homomorphism {X}\xrightarrow {{f}}{Y} of A-algebras is defined to be a function {{\mathopen {}\left \lvert X\right \rvert \mathclose {}}}\xrightarrow {{{\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}}}{{\mathopen {}\left \lvert Y\right \rvert \mathclose {}}} together with a homotopy \alpha _f \colon {\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y. A homotopy H\colon f\sim g between homomorphisms {X}\xrightarrow {{f,g}}{Y} is given by a homotopy {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\colon {\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\sim {\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}} together with an assignment to each a:A of an identification \alpha _H a\colon {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\alpha _X a \cdot \eta _g a = \eta _f a.

fr:title.text: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Structure identity principle for co-slice morphisms

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let A be a type and let {X}\xrightarrow {{f}}{Y} be a homomorphism of A-algebras. Assuming function extensionality, the type \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}g\sim f of homomorphisms homotopic to f is contractible.

We expand the dependent sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \sum _{{\mathopen {}\left (g\colon X\to Y\right )\mathclose {}}}}g\sim f as a flat record telescope:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {bmatrix*}[l] {\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}} &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert X\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\to {\mathopen {}\left \lvert Y\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\\ \alpha _g &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y\\ {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}} &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\sim {\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\\ \alpha _H &\colon &\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}} {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\alpha _X a\cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}

\begin {bmatrix*}[l] {\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}} &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert X\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\to {\mathopen {}\left \lvert Y\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\\ {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}} &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\sim {\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\\ \alpha _g &\colon &{\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\circ \alpha _X \sim \alpha _Y\\ \alpha _H &\colon &\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (a:A\right )\mathclose {}}}} {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}}\alpha _X a\cdot \alpha _g a = \alpha _f a \end {bmatrix*}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By homotopy induction (function extensionality), the first two fields contract to a point with substitution {\mathopen {}\left [{\mathopen {}\left \lvert g\right \rvert \mathclose {}}:\equiv {\mathopen {}\left \lvert f\right \rvert \mathclose {}}; {\mathopen {}\left \lvert H\right \rvert \mathclose {}}:\equiv \lambda \_.\mathsf {refl}\right ]\mathclose {}}. What remains of our telescope is the following:
Zooming into the \alpha _H component, we simplify it and then zoom back out:

By dependent homotopy induction (also function extensionality), the remaining data collapse to a point with substitution {\mathopen {}\left [\alpha _g :\equiv \alpha _f; \alpha _H :\equiv \lambda \_.\mathsf {refl}\right ]\mathclose {}}. The remaining telescope is empty, so we are done.

The steps performed in the proof of the SIP lemma for co-slice morphisms above are most naturally expressed at the level of a flat telescope (a context). The goal is always to contract a telescope to a point, and each step is either a rearrangement of the telescope, a “zooming in” step that adjusts a component of the telescope, or an application of some form of identity induction to kill off part of the telescope — path induction, homotopy induction, equivalence induction, etc. In general, we want to be able to easily point out the fragment of a telescope that contracts to a point, and then use the resulting substitution to modify the telescope and proceed further until the entire telescope is gone.

I believe that there is a reasonable and practical formal logic for doing such things, and that this logic can be implemented as a series of lemmas in homotopy type theory that can then be animated via tactics for interactive backward-inference proofs of contractibility.

Let \mathcal {I} be an identity system on an element a:A and let B be a family of types over A. A dependent identity system over \mathcal {I} on an element b:Ba in the family B consists of a family of types \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (x,q\right )\mathclose {}} over Bx for each x:A,q:\mathcal {I}x such that \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{I}\right )\mathclose {}} is equipped with the structure of an identity system on b : Ba. In particular, we have:

fr:ol.fr:li: for each family P over \mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{I}\right )\mathclose {}}, a dependent function J_{\mathcal {J}}P : \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (y:Ba\right )\mathclose {}}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (q:\mathcal {J}{\mathopen {}\left (a,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {I}}\right )\mathclose {}}y\right )\mathclose {}}}}{P{\mathopen {}\left (y,q\right )\mathclose {}}} and a dependent function \beta _{\mathcal {J}}P:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\textstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{{\mathopen {}\left (p:P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {}}}} {J_{\mathcal {J}}p\,b\,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}} =_{P{\mathopen {}\left (b,\mathsf {refl}_{\mathcal {J}}\right )\mathclose {}}} {p}.
